Patch Set 3: Code-Review-2

Many thanks to Honghui for his dedication and hard work.

The following are my comments. Please understand that the purpose is for our common goal of a stable platform (S3P), and to make a better quality of Beijing (R2) Release and future releases.

While I understand the intention is the narrowed scope of API definition, the full and normative definition of API and resources will not be considered (Line 56-58), I really worried about its impact on overall platform stability and scalability.

For example, current definition is only limited to "host" and related "hypervisor" resources, the questions are:

* how can we ensure that when we attempt to scale to other resources, not only the atomic resources but also composite resources?
* Do we have to design another spec?
* How to ensure the new specs of bigger scope is backward compatible with this spec?
* How much extra work is needed to maintain the backward compatibility?
* Your parser and validator etc. will certainly be impacted, and the numerous patches of code in long term may become much more complex than it should be if we design it well at the very beginning.

Without complete understanding of above questions, platform stability and scalability, and even the performance will be adversary impacted in the future.

Given those unknowns, I really worried.

I suggest that we de-scope it from Beijing Release (R2) in order not to have an adversary impact on not only the quality of Beijing Release but also the S3P for the future.

Instead, let us focus on the the design of a full, normative definition of API and resources in the bigger picture. Given the time constraints, the design work can be targeted for Casablanca which is more reasonable.

Once we can ratify the full API design, we can design a fully scalable YAML parser and API generator. The last step is coding. Comprehensive testing is also a necessary step.

In conclusion, let us de-scope it from Beijing, focus on full, normative API design for Casablanca, and a PoC after all design specs are agreed.

Thus I gave -2.

Patch Set 3:

Hi Bin.


I understand your concern. So the spec proposed add code in a separate path, which means the existing multicloud platform will not be affected at all. 


And I really don't want to mess this spec with "full normative api definition". Because "full normative api definition" is more like a model definition to me, and it is story. This spec is just to improve how we expose api in multicloud. I guess I have made that clear in my spec, but if not, pls let me know, I will re-word the spec.


Backward compatibility.  Since there is no such plan for "full normative api definition", I don't see backward in the picture.  And if the potential future work wants to reuse the code of this spec[1-2], you may find there is just about 2 hundreds of code. I guess it is easy for any programmer to understand and reuse them. If it is not, the discussion should be in the code itself, not in spec.


I know model driven design is great, but that is a huge work. And it is totally different from the purpose of this spec. What this spec trys to achieve, is to improve multicloud in small step. 


[1] https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/35957/
[2] https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/35637/

Patch Set 4:

Xinhui and all,

I think now the issue is the change of scope of this feature based on Honghui's clarification, in addition to my prior comments about using the correct technical methods in implementing a model-driven API framework. I don't think this new scope has ever been agreed within the project during the planning stage of Beijing Release.

Let me explain as follows.

(1) Elastic API exposure was planned and agreed as implementation of "Transforming Towards Model-Driven"

At the stage of Beijing Release planning in January, one important direction we agreed is "Transforming Towards Model-Driven", which is documented in our planning wiki page:

https://wiki.onap.org/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=22250779.

When we discussed and agreed on the feature "Elastic API exposure", it was agreed that this feature will implement the model-driven direction. Then we created JIRA-152 

https://jira.onap.org/browse/MULTICLOUD-152

under Epic "Evolve Multi Cloud towards Model-driven" (JIRA-146)

https://jira.onap.org/browse/MULTICLOUD-146

So my understanding from the very beginning has been that this feature is the implementation of model-driven API framework. Thus Honghui's clarification "totally different from model-driven" gave me a big surprise.

(2) Correct Implementation of Model-Driven

I have given my comment previously in the review history regarding how to architect and implement a correct "model-driven" architecture. I also referred to a mature industry implementation of a model-driven API framework, namely OpenStack Gluon, at project meeting and the gerrit review numerously, and expect that our implementation of model-driven API framework can go towards the right direction of implementation in order to save our time, reduce the learning curve and speed up the implementation cycle.

Unfortunately, my comment and technical concerns have never been considered seriously.

(3) We need to do the right thing in transforming and implementing model-driven API framework

In summary, my point is that we need to do the right thing in transforming and implementing model-driven architecture:

- Change of scope of this feautre is NOT a right way merely because we want to deliver something for Beijing release for the sake of being looked good.
  * All documented plan of record and discussion at project meetings indicated that this feature was agreed as the implementation of model-driven API framework
  * There is no reason of changing the scope now merely because we cannot implement the original scope for Beijing.
- We need to carefully architect and design the model-driven API framework
  * Learn from mature industry prior art. I would be happy to give a tutorial about Gluon's architecture and design.
  * Start from a full and normative definition of API resource model
  * Align with Infrastructure Resource Model when we define the API resource model in order to avoid any discrepancy, this is because those 2 models need to be consistent.

So let us do the right thing to transform to model-driven in our API framework. We can start it as a PoC towards Casablanca or even Dublin, because even in OpenStack Gluon, it got to mature through Newton, Ocata and Pike 3 release cycles.

Thank you
Bin

Patch Set 4:

Xinhui and Gildas,

Following my previous comments, more questions are:

- When we discussed and passed API Freeze (M3), have we presented accurate information? i.e.:
  * API Design of this spec has never been mature and approved.
  * There are technical issues under discussion regarding what is the best technical way of architecting and designing a model-driven API framework.
  * This technical issue has not been resolved as of today.

- Scope change:
  * All documented plan of record indicates that this feature was planned as a model-driven API framework
  * Now the clarification indicates “totally different from model-driven”. I consider it as the change of scope.
  * Since we have passed M2 Feature Freeze, is it appropriate to change the scope of this feature at this moment?

Appreciate if you can respond.

Thank you
Bin

Patch Set 5:

This spec is about how we expose API. I have the same consensus that full model-driven is a huge work that may take several releases. But there are many good things in model-driven, maybe we can adopt some at one time. For example, how model driven exposes REST API. From code implementation perspective, I do believe it is an more advanced approach, because developer doesn’t need to copy/paste code. All common code are extracted together. Developer only needs to define how the api should looks like.
So, basically, this spec just offers an framework for API exposure, by referencing the approach of model-driven. I would see it as a baby step toward model-driven. Since we have this baby step forward, when the real full model definition comes, it would be easier to move forward.
Besides, take the ceilometer API that VoLTE uses as an example, the cloud monitor might be ceilometer for OpenStack, or it is possible to be other cloud monitor backend. By using this approach, we can easily switch different cloud monitor, by applying different yaml files.

So, I think this spec is good for Multi-Cloud. I know there is some ongoing discussion about full normative model definition. But I don’t think we should block other good things into Multi-Cloud just because one big topic is not finished.

Removed the following votes:

* Code-Review-2 by Bin Hu <bh526r@att.com>

Patch Set 7:

Thank you Honghui for your time, and comments.

I have uploaded a new patch https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/38147/ for further review and discussion.

All reviewers: please go to the patch https://gerrit.onap.org/r/#/c/38147/ and review and comment.

Regarding your initial questions:

Line 23: That's my typo. Sorry. It is not code generation, but generating API endpoints and related data schema for API objects in runtime. I changed it in new patch.

Line 39: Every example is for exemplary purpose. I understand it is confusing, and post/put/delete of a host extends current capability. So I changed it to another example to avoid confusion. 

The focus is the definition syntax and structure itself. Full example is still subject to further development, as mentioned at the end of this document. And the full model also needs to align with infrastructure data model, which is still at very early stage.

Line 332: I changed the example to avoid confusion. So the focus now is the definition syntax and structure. Full content of the model needs to align with infrastructure data model, which is at early stage.
Change has been successfully merged by zhang ab
